text: You are in for a ride!! I started the Children of Time series after finishing the Cantos. For a good 2-3 days initially I could not concentrate on Children of Time and kept mixing up characters or scenes from the Cantos in my imagination.

text: These books are some of the worst I’ve ever read and I’ve read some doozies. Still, it amazes me how others I know that share similar tastes find them amazing. Kudos to Simmons, he could come up with interesting concepts and create memorable characters, even if he couldn’t weave a coherent plot or stop himself from writing pretentious love letters to Keats and others throughout his works.
label: Opinion / Hot Take

text: The Day of the Triffids is a classic sci-fi. Instead of becoming entranced, all the lookers become blind, and there are other endangering circumstances. The book follows someone who didnt look. So not quite your topic, but some of the themes.

text: Historically (not saying that its true) I want to say there was something in Lourdes and maybe something to do with Emperor Constantine, who claimed to see the Constantine Cross (in this sign I will conquer) both involving people seeing things in the sky, though I dont know about a trance.

text: Starting off, for some reason I believe romance is generally considerably better written in sci-fi than the likes of fantasy, comedies, etc. Maybe its the beautiful vistas that otherwordly planets can afford, or the unique character traits that other genres can't realistcally cover.

text: Imagining possible futures, both good and bad. Stretching imaginations. “Sensawunder“ (sense of wonder). Encouraging outside-the-box thinking. Examining the human condition in circumstances that don’t presently obtain.

text: What I always liked is that scifi can turn abstract ideas or philosophical theories into narratives that explain a difficult idea in a much more intuitive way. You probably still don't fully understand how it works, but you know what it looks and feels like

text: I played the Westwood RTS games before I saw the movies or read the book so I was so confused. The games premise is that the Houses fight each other for territory on Arrakis in a sort of giant gladiatorial fight and as long as they give the spice they harvest in the territory they control to the emperor they get money and favour. Not at all like the book where its just a transfer of a fiefdom gone bloody.
